ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tamagoyaki; closest parent is omelette food product (FOODON_03302050).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 term exists specifically for tamagoyaki; closest parent is cooked hen eggs (A00LC). Tamagoyaki is a sweetened, layered rolled omelette made from hen eggs.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for tamagoyaki or Japanese rolled omelette.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of tamagoyaki sushi at a Japanese sushi restaurant consists of 3 pieces, each piece comprising a slice of rolled egg omelette atop a small mound of seasoned sushi rice, totalling approximately 90 g.

nutrient_source.title: Egg, whole, cooked, scrambled / omelette — adapted to tamagoyaki composition; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy Item 01132 and Japanese Standard Tables of Food Composition (MEXT 2020) entry for tamagoyaki (玉子焼き)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Japan (MEXT)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values are drawn from the Japanese Standard Tables of Food Composition 2020 (MEXT), entry for tamagoyaki (item code 12-0012), which reports values per 100 g of the cooked product. USDA SR Legacy egg data used for cross-validation of protein and fat values. The MEXT table is the authoritative source for Japanese prepared egg dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — egg-based omelette / egg dishes analogous estimate; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tamagoyaki in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 46 is estimated by analogy with cooked egg dishes and sweetened egg preparations reported in Atkinson et al. (2021) and the Sydney GI Database. The carbohydrate content of tamagoyaki is primarily from added sugar and mirin; egg protein and fat substantially blunt the glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (46 × 10.35) / 100 ≈ 4.7, where carbohydrate per 90 g serving = 11.5 × 0.9 = 10.35 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and dashi
hypertension.guidance: Tamagoyaki contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g, primarily from soy sauce and dashi seasoning; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 1–2 pieces and choose low-sodium soy sauce variants where available.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar and mirin contributing to carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tamagoyaki contains approximately 11.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, largely from added sugar and mirin; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should account for this carbohydrate contribution within their meal plan and prefer smaller portions to manage postprandial glucose response, noting the low GI of approximately 46 is partially mitigated by the sugar source.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary cholesterol and saturated fat from eggs
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tamagoyaki provides approximately 270 mg cholesterol and 2.3 g saturated fat per 100 g from whole eggs; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume in moderation as part of a diet that keeps total dietary cholesterol within clinically recommended limits, and should be mindful of the sodium contribution from soy sauce seasoning.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tamagoyaki (玉子焼き) is a traditional Japanese rolled omelette made by cooking multiple thin layers of seasoned beaten egg in a rectangular tamagoyaki pan (makiyakinabe), rolling each layer over the previous one to form a compact, layered log shape. The seasoning typically includes soy sauce, mirin, sugar, and dashi stock, giving the omelette a distinctively sweet-savoury flavour profile and a soft, slightly springy texture. When served as sushi (tamago nigiri or tamagoyaki sushi), a slice of the rolled omelette is placed atop a small hand-pressed mound of vinegared sushi rice and sometimes secured with a thin strip of nori. In the context of the input record, it is served as 3-piece tamagoyaki sushi at Sushi Express in Singapore. Nutritionally, tamagoyaki is a moderate-energy food at approximately 151 kcal per 100 g, providing nearly 10 g of high-quality protein and 7.8 g of fat per 100 g, along with 11.5 g of carbohydrate largely from added sugar and mirin. It is a meaningful source of vitamin B12, vitamin A, riboflavin, and dietary cholesterol. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 46 (low GI), though the added sugar content means carbohydrate management is relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium content of approximately 310 mg per 100 g warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. Tamagoyaki is not suitable for vegans and is not halal-certified in most commercial preparations due to mirin (rice wine) content. It is a staple of Japanese bento boxes, sushi restaurants, and home cooking across Japan and Japanese cuisine globally.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tamagoyaki sushi?
What is tamago sushi made of?
What is the GI of tamagoyaki?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from the Japanese Standard Tables of Food Composition 2020 (MEXT), which is an authoritative national food composition database for Japan and provides a direct entry for tamagoyaki. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct GI measurement for tamagoyaki exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero per MEXT 2020 as tamagoyaki contains no plant-based fibre sources. Vitamin C is 0.0 g per 100 g; confirmed zero as eggs contain negligible vitamin C and no vitamin-C-rich ingredients are used. Trans fat is 0.0 g per 100 g; confirmed zero per MEXT 2020 — eggs contain naturally negligible industrial trans fats. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and all micronutrient values are drawn from MEXT 2020 tamagoyaki entry and USDA SR Legacy egg data. Halal status is marked false because traditional tamagoyaki uses mirin, which contains alcohol; halal-certified variants exist but are not the default. The serving size of 90 g (3 pieces) is estimated based on standard sushi restaurant portion conventions; individual piece weight approximately 30 g including rice base. GI of 46 is an analogous estimate — no direct tamagoyaki GI measurement was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date; the estimate is based on egg-based cooked dishes and the blunting effect of protein and fat on glycaemic response as documented in Atkinson et al. (2021), Diabetes Care. GL per serving = (46 × 10.35) / 100 ≈ 4.7. Sentinel values: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported as positive numeric values from MEXT 2020 and are not sentinel -1 values. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record as MEXT 2020 provides coverage for all reported fields.
